turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I -- I think that he said this, that he asked Mr. Meade, who is the County Clerk, "Was there any other record?" And Mr. Meade said, I don't have any other record that would indicate and don't I think --

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What did --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- he went any -- I don't think he searched any further than that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Who did have it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Who did have another record?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I believe the Sheriff's office.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that a public record?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Required by the statute to be kept in the Sheriff's Office?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that statute quoted in your brief?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I don't have that statute but the statute on summoning the jurors, they have to have a summons with their name on it and their address which is kept from the record in the Sheriff's office, and I say this, that this record could have been complete with a little more -- if he had just gotten that in then we'd have a complete picture of it. And I'll confess, I'm as ignorant of the true facts as anyone, but I do know this, that this list are the number that he say actually served, actually appeared that's true, I'll accept his testimony on that, but I'll say this, it is not the actual number who were selected.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Does the record show that he tried to prove this through the Sheriff's office and --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- the judge wouldn't permit him to do it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor it was the same record that -- that he had searched.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: It was the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: It was the record from the clerk's --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- office. Sir?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Wasn't that a particular record, they wanted him to consult the records and find out how many it was, and the judge said that'll take too much time, we can't do that, you do it, you've made an investigation of these records, you use -- you use --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He had --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- your information.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: The judge --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, he's trying to get it from the Sheriff, didn't he?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What did he try to get?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That the record that he was attempting to have this other gentleman testify from, was the record from the County Clerk's office which is the identical record that he had searched and already knew the numbers, so in order to expedite the trial the judge asked him haven't you searched the same record and he said, "Yes." "Well, put to testimony on by yourself otherwise we'll have to sit here while this man reads some 40 pages of records."

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did the State make any objection to this (Inaudible) the record?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) May I ask you now, you -- you know the system, have you any reason to tell us some (Inaudible) that the other record would make any substantial difference?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I think that it would possibly maybe not over two or three more on each panel. I wouldn't venture more than that.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You think it might possibly show that two or three were summoned but didn't come.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true. And it might show four, five or six, white that didn't come too?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Probably about the same percentage (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I would think so.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible) a little more than white.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I doubt it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You do?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Have you any -- have you -- do you happen to have any light on why from 1947 to 1955, figures on the whole run pretty uniformly except in (Inaudible) there are considerable variances, 64 (Inaudible) the total number of jurors is white, 64 in 1950, 54 in 1951, (Inaudible) and then 65, 1958. Which is considered the smallest of the number, the percentage of increases from the average is enormous rather considerably. Do you happen to have any reasons why that in March (Inaudible) there were 64, in the October term, 49 -- I mean, 32.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: There is nothing in the record --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To the --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- for me to base --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) got anything to do with it, this October? I noticed that -- that is true because two of the increased numbers happen to be in October, two in March and (Inaudible)

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you summon a bigger panel when you have capital cases (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Ordinarily if it --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You have elected --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- if there's a lot of feeling about it, the answer would be yes, they would summon more.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are there more before there's more peremptory challenges. Well, you got to have more (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's -- that's true and then of course if a perspective juror on his voir dire examination would say that he does believe in capital punishment, why, he'd be crossed off and so it does take a greater amount of veniremen for a capital case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't have a special panel?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't have the special panel?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: We have special, what we call special panels that are picked by the jury commissioners at the same time they select the grand jury and the petit jury.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I mean (Inaudible) capital cases we try, can you summon a greater number of jurors, prospective jurors for that venire.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's a special venire.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes. There are always -- a list is always available and then of course if something like this is coming up then they can't summon. But I think possibly that this case stands pretty much in the same situation, that United States against Brant in the Northern District of Ohio which was a 1955 case, 139 F. Supp. where we are making the same claim as they are making here that Negroes as a class were excluded from jury service. However, in the Brant case the record was clear that throughout the years covered by the evidence colored persons were represented on the jury list and actually served as jurors in the cases and that's the same thing that we have here. The Court said to sustain this claim. Defendant's relied in part on the fact that no member of the indicting grand jury was a Negro that their contention that this evidence -- that this is evidence of discrimination is void of merit, because it's well settled and an accused person is not entitled to have a jury composed and in part of persons who are members of the his own race nor is anyone entitled to that. And then they cite the Martin against Texas in 200 US and Akins against Texas in 325 U.S. Now to -- I think that the Akins case is probably the complete answer to this one. And the language of this Court in that case is that petitioner's sole objection to the grand jury is that the commissioners deliberately and intentionally and purposefully limited number of the Negro race that should be selected on same said -- grand jury panel to one member. Now, that's what he is saying here that that we are systematic -- we have systematic inclusion rather than systematic exclusion. And this Court said fairness in selection has never been held to require proportional representation of races upon the jury citing Virginia against Rives and Thomas against Texas. This Court further said our direct --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The other side of this --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The other side of this involves of any such -- trying to maintain the said principle, don't they?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I am sure that's true.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Inaudible) as I understood Mr. Branton, he said he didn't make any such contention on his brief, (Inaudible) The brief excludes that.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But I mean, I think what he is saying though that the fact that the number of Negroes were so small on the jury panels that that would show inclusion, systematic inclusion but to follow that line of reasoning further then the only way a state could ever prove that it -- there was not systematic inclusion would -- then you'd have to go the proportionate basis.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Systematic inclusion isn't allowed, isn't it, the legal doctrine, all that was meant (Inaudible) that you do not satisfy -- do not disapprove prohibitive discrimination by, saying, “Well, we'll have one Negro or two.” In any event that will -- that will satisfy the requirement or disprove on this way the systematic discrimination. You can't -- he wouldn't object having systematically included more Negroes than -- than anybody mathematically required (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He seems to be objecting though because we are not --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He wasn't objecting that -- he wouldn't be here though we've had a case there are (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, he seems to be objecting though Your Honor that we haven't included enough.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that -- that's a discrimination, that the inclusion of the (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But it's still the State's contention that he has not met his burden of proof because we don't know how many. There are actually -- that they were actually selected and the only way we can arrive at any figure on that would be by speculation and conjecture because --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible) you do not disapprove this claim of intentional discrimination by saying, “Well look, each term is quite so -- that each term we did that, one Negro, do not satisfy the principle of inadmissible exclusion by including a (Inaudible) colored man?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: When a -- Your Honor, I believe --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not saying that this -- I am not saying that this -- that this case, I'm saying that its position, your answer is that he has a burden of showing no matter what the figures show that the figures are of the result with the intention of keeping out colored people from service, who shouldn't be allowed to be on the panel.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: And I think to prove, to prove his claim then he must show how many were actually selected, not how many actually served, which is all his record shows here. And if it were like you, your example there just one every time then I'd agree with you that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Why wouldn't it -- why would this -- that settled it. People can be (Inaudible) in such a way that those selected could know in advance they would serve and that has come up (Inaudible) the result. Why therefore do you say that that wouldn't -- you have to show the number that you've selected.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well I mean if you are ever going to prove that discrimination then I think you must show the actual numbers that were selected because the discrimination lies in the selection rather than in the serving.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Suppose -- suppose this thing could show -- suppose the whites were -- they haven't made those (Inaudible) Would you say that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I'd say that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- that that's discrimination?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I'd say the petitioner had a much stronger case --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, you -- would you still say think that nobody could infer from that in the record?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: You mean from a --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: No, no -- (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: The number that we are actually served rather than from the number that was selected?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I'm afraid that I'd probably have to infer that there was some discrimination.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And suppose that -- but supposed it's reversed the other way that a number of white people had served with (Inaudible) A number of colored people would (Inaudible) how would you feel about that? Is that your discrimination?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: You mean, with the same number of qualified electors?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- just like in here.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Probably wouldn't. I'd like to briefly discuss the petitioner's confessions. I believe Mr. Justice Frankfurter said to -- set out the grounds that -- believed that it was coerced. Well, the petitioner's grounds, he alleges one that he was mentally retarded, that he was arrested without a warrant, that he was interrogated constantly, that he was deprived food, threatened by mobs and that he was held incommunicado and that the Chief of Police had threatened to arrest all the members of his family if he didn't confess. Now, if all these things were true then I would not hesitate to tell this Court they should reverse it because in my mind it would coercion. But the only thing that I know to do in explaining these things is go to the record and see what the testimony is. And Mr. Branton -- I believe all of the facts as he gave them was based on the testimony of the petitioner and since he was on trial for his life he might be excused some latitude in his testimony. Now, to go to the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I want to -- I wanted (Inaudible) Mr. Justice Frankfurter asked you this (Inaudible) what he considers the undisputed facts but were -- but it can be shown towards you. Would it serve your argument if you were to state what you conceded would be fact, undisputed fact insofar as the confession is -- was at first (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: All right, there's only one and that is a testimony of the Chief of Police when he told the petitioner he was the -- the Chief of Police was on cross-examination, was asked by Mr. Branton, “Did you tell the petitioner that 30 or 40 people were likely to be there in a few minutes? And he said, “I said there could possibly be that many and there possibly would be.” Now, I frankly and in all truthfulness do not know whether he meant they were coming there to witness the confession, that they were -- that he told them there was a mob outside, the record to me is not clear but that is the only questionable incident --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: How long was that --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- in this whole record --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: How long was that supposed confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: About five minutes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Record 69, it's about 12 lines down, question is, “State whether or not you told him, the defendant, there might be 30 or 40 people there in the next few minutes?” Answer “I said it was possible.” Now, to me that is the only piece of evidence --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What -- now what -- where is the -- the piece of evidence where the defendant said he was told there might be 30 or 40 people there?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's on record page 69.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I mean, the petitioner, they have -- they have said -- stating there'd be 30 or 40 people there.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, now this --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What did he say was the reason?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: What did the petitioner say, he said that there was -- that the Chief of Police told him that there were 30 or 40 people that wanted to get in there to get him, I believe is the way he put it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was the Chief of Police -- did he make any further statements in this -- about it at all?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: To my knowledge this is at -- on page 69.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is -- could you quickly turn to the testimony of the petitioner on this very point, what is his comment of these 30 or 40 people?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I believe 128.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: At the bottom of the page. Question “What caused you to be afraid, Frank?” Answer “Well, it was the conversation that Chief Norman Young was talking, when he said, 30 or 40 peoples outside.” Then from the conversation of this officer down at Dumas and the way he was talking, in other words. He says peoples, it were somebody outside and were someone that was outside wanting to get in to me.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This officer down at Dumas, that's a different --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That happened a different night, the record is clear on that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't that -- and that you say were --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: This, but this --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that -- is there a -- is that also unquestioned, is there also agreement on that?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But only as to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As to what --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: - what is in the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) of the confession, is that it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: What happened at Dumas is fairly, clearly happened a night later. There is -- there is just, I mean the evidence in the record is just abundant on that.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: After the confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: What occurred at Dumas --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Was after the confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Was after the confession. I don't think there is any question about that and I have the record cited to show what happened after the confession at 58, 60, 66, 90, 91 and 106. Now, at record 91, now this is petitioner testifying, he forgot himself here and he -- what was this statement the man said you about hanging somebody? Answer “And the petitioner has testified and he has mental lapse here, he wasn't on guard, while he was questioning me, as I said before, he was questioning me, had I confessed that I did it, and I told him, yes, I had.”

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, at -- on page -- at the top of page 128 --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I believe he said, with reference to the same conversation that the Chief testified about -- Chief of Police, Chief Norman Young said 30 or 40 people were outside wanting to get in to me. And he asked me if I wanted to make a confession he would try to keep them out. Did any -- did the state ask the Chief whether or not that was true?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Page 69.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Page 69.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I believe it was the -- Mr. Branton was cross-examining the Chief on that point.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But where did he deny that.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: At -- that state whether or not -- it's about the -- about 12 lines down on page 69. “State whether or not you told him, the defendant, there might be 30 or 40 people there in the next few minutes.” And Chief Young answered, “I said it was possible.”

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, that pretty plainly refers to the statement that this man made that he said there were 30 or 40 people out there waiting to get him, doesn't it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well now, it goes on here is the reason that I have a question about --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did -- did the state ever ask that the policeman ever deny it, Chief of Police ever deny it that other parts of the statement that he made about the 30 or 40 people?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: To my knowledge he didn't, this is it. He just says, “I said it was possible.” And I think another place in the record somewhere, he was testifying about it and he says, “I said it was possible there could be that many.” And he said, “It was possible there would have been that many.”

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you say that's the --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's the Chief of Police.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) as I understood you, you say that is according to your view and I should just apply the question, that the only thing that is -- as to which there is agreement between the petitioner --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, that's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that the State couldn't, isn't that right?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's -- that's the only thing I think that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, that result --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- that you might infer any -- any course from --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There will be (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That would be the only statement in the whole record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, and there's no controversy that the Chief of Police said something about 30 or 40 people in the next few minutes, is that right?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But then he goes on right on page 69 --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Nobody -- nobody every denied the statement of petitioner that the Chief of Police told him that they were out there to get him.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: What I -- I wish you would note Mr. Justice Black, how this question is phrased on page 69. You'll notice that he doesn't mention anything that 30 or 40 people to go in there to get you, he said there'd be 30 or 40 people there in a few minutes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Now, there were several people --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But you'd have a --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- that did come in after this confession who witnessed it. Now, I don't personally know and can't gain from the record whether the -- the Chief was referring to the witnesses or he actually did say something about a mob.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But you -- the fact remains, does it not, that you have a statement by the petitioner that he said -- he said there were 30 or 40 people, that he said they were there to get him, you have no denial of that in the record by anybody representing Arkansas.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I believe that's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, that isn't quite regarding the testimony because the action to include not only the connotation that Chief Norman Young but also of that officer at Dumas, can they --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, I believe you're on a different page.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm talking about page 128.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, I was talking about page 69.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that, that when a (Inaudible) I understand that the chief of your argument is the Chief of Police simply says, 30 people there in the next few minutes without saying what the people were there for, what their purpose was, that is correct, isn't it? That's your point.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's -- that's true.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, what I'm -- I'm turning to what the petitioner has made on page 28 -- 128. And the petitioner gets in the phrase to get -- to get into me, he concludes that act referring not only what Chief Norman Young said there, but also what the officer down at Dumas said.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's at the bottom of the page.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But what about the top of the page?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- at the bottom of page 128.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What about the top of the page, is that --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Only the part says, wanting to get in to.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, has that been disputed by you here? He would try to keep them out, has that been -- has that been disputed by you?

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: In your brief on page 11 counsel of your brief a few lines from the bottom of the page. You state it as apparently what Chief Young meant was there would be other people there to witness the confession. Now, where is that?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That is what I get out from page 69 because when you come on down and read in context without taking it out of context. If you take it out of context, it looks -- you don't know. But, if you go on down close to the bottom of -- that's the --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: But the thing that's wrong with that is that when somebody did knock on the door to want to come in, he wouldn't let anybody in, even though he was the Sheriff to witness the confession.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true, he -- he had -- he -- in his testimony, the Sheriff said, I had promised Frank, the petitioner, that he could make this confession to me in private and he kept his promise and he wouldn't let anyone else in, he testified until he got Frank's permission.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: So that -- that all adds up against what you say in the bottom of page 11 as to the reason that he -- he would.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I don't think so if you read it in context. On 69, right after he says, “I said it was possible.” “Did you go downstairs to your office?” “Yes.” “Was his confession interrupted?” Says, “It was by a knock on the door,” and he went to the door, he said the then Sheriff and a State Trooper were at the door. “Did you permit them to come in?” "I did not.” “Did you tell them anything about the defendant?” He said, “I told them that he was making an oral confession.” And said then, “Did he complete his confession roughly, then what you do?” “I asked him if he would mind the Sheriff and Sergeant Halsell to come in.” “How much later was that?” He says, “Not more than five minutes.” Question “Then, did the other people come in?” Connect it back up here, there would be 30 or 40 there, reading it directly in context.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: So what you have here, I wish you'd state whether or not you think that shows the confession was coerced or not.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I do not.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, let me ask a question. You have undisputed testimony to this effect, no witnesses deny it. Chief Norman Young said 30 or 40 people were outside waiting to get in to me, and he asked me if I wanted to make a confession, he would try to keep them out. And nobody has denied that. In your judgment, if that is true, was this a free and voluntary confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I think it was.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Even with that statement?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: For -- for this reason.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Even with that statement made?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I don't think possibly the statement as you put it was not denied as such, but during Chief Young's testimony, he explained it in a different way, I mean answered the questions in a different way where you could possibly draw any inference you want to, you --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: He made no statement on (Inaudible) about whether this man -- whether 30 or 40 people there, the statement was true, that he had told this young fellow that there were 30 to 40 people out there waiting to get him.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But the reason that I think that it wasn't coerced is his subsequent demeanor after that. That is shown by the testimony of the court reporter and the other witnesses to the effect that, well, a few minutes later, here he is testifying, and he was watching the court reporter to takes the notes in shorthand, and he is very careful not to talk too fast, so she'll get everything down that he is saying.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: And he shows no outward signs of fear, and that would lead me to believe that there is just no substance to --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But you do have --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- the argument of a coerced confession.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But you do have to defend it, do you not, on the basis that this undisputed testimony in the record. The Chief of Police told him five minutes before the confession, you got to go ahead and talk to the 30 or 40 out there waiting to get you.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, it depends on whose testimony you're going to believe, the Chief of Police --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, who denies it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: They didn't deny it as such.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Thomas before you sit down, what have you got to say about the proof, the lack of proof? Now as I understood Mr. Branton, as I understand the briefs, this man was arrested at about 10 o'clock Wednesday morning. He was given no food on Wednesday, he was given the sandwich, I think, your witnesses say two sandwiches on -- at noon on Thursday, and then nothing more to eat until after he -- after he confessed on Friday afternoon. Now, what do you have to say about that?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: There is no testimony in the record on behalf of the State that shows whether he was fed or not. The -- Chief Young testified that the first meal in the morning at the jail was at 8, the evening meal started around between 4 and 5.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And they took him by 6 o'clock to Little Rock.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, sir, but --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: So he had no breakfast.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: The -- the record positively shows that he was fed two sandwiches or at least one, by his own testimony, at noon that day.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That after he came --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: 26 hours (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: After they had finished taken the lie detector --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: 26 hours after he was arrested.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, he was arrested on --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: At 10 o'clock on Wednesday morning (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: 11 o'clock on Wednesday --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- or about noon on -- on Thursday as I understood it.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He was arrested on Wednesday, the sandwiches were on Thursday noon --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thursday (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- when -- that was after he had completed the lie detector test, and was turned back over to the police officer. They fed him and returned him to Pine Bluff, where he was placed in jail for the remainder of the night, which would indicate to me that he was there at the time that the food was served. Certainly he was there the next morning, because he wasn't taken out of that cell until he wanted to confess at approximately 1 o'clock on Friday.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But he testified -- he testified that he did not receive any food and although the State took notice of that, all they would say was that he was in the building when the others were fed.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I don't know whether they could prove it Your honor or not. As I say I wasn't in that phase of the case, I have only been connected with it during the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But that's all that -- that that's (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But there is nothing -- I will say that very frankly there's nothing in the record any direct testimony that would reflect anything to the contrary other than what the petitioner said.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Well now do you not -- do you not think that a 19-year-old man, whatever you want to call him or a boy, whichever it is, who was thrown into jail and held for 26 hours, without any food, and then is given one or two sandwiches. And then this questions off and on for another 26 hours or so before he's given any more food. And half of the time without even any shoes on, dragged around the country. Do you not think that that has some effect upon his -- his mental condition and his willingness to -- to do what the police wanted to do?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, Your Honor may I say you this.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I -- reading the record, and staying strictly with the record, I have not drawn the same conclusions from this that you have, like you say --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But what good --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- where he is dragged around the contrary without any shoes, he was merely taken from Pine Bluff to Little Rock without shoes where he was given shoes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, but as I read the record, your own witnesses said that his shoes were taken from him about 6:30 in the morning and then they took him to Little Rock and they gave him some shoes about 4:30 in the afternoon.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, the officer testified that -- Officer Halsell testified that he gave him the shoes immediately upon his arrival at State Police Headquarters that morning. Now, it was the petitioner who said that he didn't receive the shoes until 4:30 in the afternoon.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, I made a mistake about (Inaudible)

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But I say according to the record --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- if the facts were as you stated to me, then my answer would have been different.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, let's just -- let's leave the shoes out of it, and just take that food conversation. Do you not -- do you think that that to his conduct compatible with free and voluntary confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I don't think that one factor alone would affect it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No. Well, how about -- how about (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's because I'm very (Inaudible) myself voluntarily.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How about that factor coupled with the other one, to the effect that the Sheriff told him that there were 30 or 40 people out there and he tried to protect him. Would that be --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well now, on the -- on the morning -- on that Friday morning, I -- I don't believe there was any dispute in a record that he was placed back in the jail on his return from Little Rock, for the remainder of the night. They had to put him there the next morning. I mean, he had to be in jail the next morning during the time they served breakfast.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well that doesn't prove that they gave him any breakfast.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And there's -- there is no one for the State who testified that they did give him any breakfast.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: There's no one who testified that any if at all except those two sandwiches 26 hours after he was arrested.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But by his own testimony he said the reason I confessed is because I was afraid of what the Sheriff told me and what the jailer at Dumas told me. He never once says in his testimony that the reason he confessed was because he was hungry.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, he testified the fact that he didn't get any food, didn't he?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, he said he had two sandwiches and the coke and a candy bar and some peanuts and testified that Sergeant Halsell, although he had had breakfast, now this was in Dumas that morning in jail, Sergeant Halsell, he told him he was still hungry and he stopped outside of Dumas and bought him ham and eggs and milk.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Ham and eggs?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: When was that, after his defense?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well that was -- apparently after -- that was after he had confessed, that was at Dumas.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. Well, that makes a difference, doesn't it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But he also fed him, the day that he had him in Little Rock, of course there is no -- it is like you say there is no testimony to the contrary, to contradict that he said he wasn't fed.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well then, that's the reason why I -- I asked you whether there isn't more than just the one point, you say the only issue in the case, as you see it is what the Sherriff is supposed to have told him just beforehand. But you don't attach any significance to the fact that this young was kept in jail and questioned and night and day at various times, I don't say continuously, and that he was given no food, except one or two sandwiches over a period of more than two day.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, the record will reflect that he was given more then just the two sandwiches.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well when -- where -- where --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: By his own testimony, he says in there that besides that that he was given a candy bar, that he was given some peanuts and on his own testimony he said that the jailer threatened him in Dumas before he confessed and that he goes in all that that he was fed before he was confessed. Of course, that isn't true because --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And you tell us that those things happened afterwards so the --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's true --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- the statement about -- about the --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: But he still received -- he --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- (Voice Overlap) about getting the time he got that candy and so forth in Dumas.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No. He didn't say he got it in Dumas, he said he got it in the jail.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) ham and eggs for breakfast on Friday morning.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Sergent Halsell purchased that for him outside of Dumas.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, what was the defense on the merits?

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Could I ask --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: I -- I don't quite follow you Judge Whittaker.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, did -- he denied guilt on the finding of the case, the defense on the merits that --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No, no. He -- he admitted that he killed the man. But he said he --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: What was the defense?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He said he did it in a sudden heat of passion.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: That doesn't appear in this record, I (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No, Your -- no --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: -- typewritten record this morning and I looked at it hastily, am I --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: There is much at this recording --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Am I correct in thinking that he took the stand in his own defense apart from the confession, he admitted that he killed the man and that the defense was that he had done it in self defense, is that correct?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Self defense and sudden heat of passion, there were two --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Heat of passion.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: (Inaudible) there.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: But he never repudiated his confession on the witness stand which he did -- where he testified in his own behalf, did he?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He said the -- I believe that that wasn't the truth, and that he was telling the truth when he testified in his behalf, that he did repudiate the confession.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, I don't understand that statement. Where does the confession deviate from his testimony in his affirmative defense?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: It may -- it may not -- you mean, how does his testimony differ from his plea of --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: It -- of his confession?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: It doesn't itself in that sense. As I remember the record, he said that the confession he made was not true and the prosecuting attorney said, “What is the truth?” And on objection by Mr. Branton the Court stopped the prosecutor from pursuing that line of questioning any further. And I believe that testimony was taken in chambers possibly at around 91.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, was there testimony before (Inaudible) on the affirmative defense that petitioner slew his employer?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, but it is not in this record.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: It's not in this record.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: It's in the typewritten record that you haven't --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: But you haven't printed that part of the testimony in that --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's -- that's true.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: -- typewritten record to get the whole picture of what this fellow testified to.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's -- that's correct.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: You've got to look at the typewritten record --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: -- is that it?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is the -- is the $400 of the employer's money that was found in petitioner's home, is that -- this evidence -- is that in this record?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes, it is in this one and the other one too and the typewritten, it's in both of them.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he admit or deny he got the money?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Well, he took the officer's -- very frankly admitted that he took it, he took them to where he had it hidden in a piano.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You're talking about the confession.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: And also there's some other testimony and I don't know whether it's in this particular record or in the typewritten.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Well, this typewritten testimony, this typewritten record, he admitted that he took the money, didn't he?

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN II
turns.text: Testified affirmative of having taken the money.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He even took them, the officers to the spot that he thrown the iron rod where he crushed his employer's skull and point out this is where I threw it and that's where a man had found it. There was an ample independent evidence which he can -- been convicted of --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: There's ample (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: -- other than the confession.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: The ample independent evidence was that he denied that he had made the confession that they offered against him.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: No, he did never deny making the confession.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I mean as they stated it.

turns.name: THORP THOMAS
turns.text: He said that that wasn't the truth, but I don't think he -- in any, in the other record the typewritten record, he tells his story that is different than the confession. Thank you.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Branton, you may -- you may finish if you wish (Inaudible) want to come back tomorrow.

turns.name: WILEY A. BRANTON
turns.text: May it please the Court. I noticed that there has been considerable questioning back and forth as to what the Chief of Police meant about whether or not there were 30 or 40 people downstairs just prior to the confession. I wish to point out to the Court that on bottom of page 113 on cross-examination of the Chief of Police by myself, I specifically asked him this question, “State whether or not anything was said to the defendant to the effect that there would be 30 or 40 people there in a few minutes that wanted to get him? His answer was, “I told him that would be possible, that would be that many. It was possible there could be that many.” I think that clears up what the Chief of Police meant because certainly the question was, “Did you tell him that there'd be that many people there to get him.” Now the State contends what he meant was, there'd be 30 or 40 people there to witness the confession. And certainly to use the language to get him, it can't mean but one thing namely just what it implied that there would be possible mob violence. The defendant specifically repudiates his confession on page 94. It does not appear in the transcript of record printed here as to the defense on the merits for the simple reason that the only part of the record here is that part designated by the petitioner. Naturally, the petitioner was only going to designate that part that this Court would consider on certiorari, we didn't feel that we should make our state's case, they failed to cross designate, it's not here. The defendant took the witness stand on his own behalf admitted to the slaying, but states that he was working there, that he and his employer got into a very heated argument that the employer slapped him and that he reached down, passed an iron bar, and that he jumped over this counter, that they fought over, he got it away from him and beat him with an iron bar and when he realized what he had done that his first thought was to get away and the man was there checking up and he took some money. Now it should be remembered that this defendant was not charged with a crime of murder in the first degree while in the perpetration of a felony, because the State would have had -- would have had an easier case in the Arkansas law, no premeditation being required. Now, they're trying to imply here that he was charged with robbing the man. But he wasn't even charged with that. It's true that he had 400 some odd dollars but he was never charged with a slaying while the perpetration of a felony. And that is significant.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Did that testimony (Inaudible) $400 from the deceased? (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILEY A. BRANTON
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) and the testimony went in.

turns.name: WILEY A. BRANTON
turns.text: If Your Honor please, we object to the information in the first place because --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, did you object to the inference that the $400 of the deceased money was found in the home of the deceased --

turns.name: WILEY A. BRANTON
turns.text: We objected --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- of the petitioner.

turns.name: WILEY A. BRANTON
turns.text: We objected to the testimony of Sergent Halsell who first testified, yes, sir we did. But if -- if you have reference to the objection of the possible commission of other crimes insofar as that angle is concerned we did not raise that as a legal question. As to that payroll record, the payroll record is -- the payroll record accurately reflects the actual number of persons who served on the jury in Jefferson County. And therefore in answer to a question as to the number of Negroes who served the preceding year, one commissioner said that he couldn't use all of those who had been used a year before. The record shows that only two or perhaps four had been used a year before. Anybody who had been excused would not be on the payroll record and therefore could be used on the next panel. The testimony as to that payroll record is uncontradicted. The State says if we don't know how to add and that the total number there is 640, we still get 669. We attempted to put the sheriff on the stand, the record, the permanent record is kept not in the Sheriff's office, but in the office of the circuit clerk the same person who said he had no such record. And the Court didn't even want us to burden the trial because after all we had been in court for half a day and if you take longer than two days in Arkansas to electrocute a Negro, why then, you have an unusually long trial. So we wound up in two days. And the Court didn't want this trial delayed and suggested that I get on the stand and testify. Now we submit that after having been invited to make this testimony and certainly we have the burden, then the burden of rebutting that presumption which we had raised was on the State and they offered no testimony whatsoever to rebut the evidence which we had offered. As to whether or not there were more Negroes excused from jury service as compared with white, the record is silent. As I say, we'd have go outside the record but certainly the percentage of Negroes who served would be even more disparaging if we used the accurate record rather than the payroll record. And even if that was not true there is nothing here that the State says that people get excused for two things, having made this, and this, that and the other. And I don't think that this Court can take any judicial knowledge of the fact or be presumptuous enough to say that Negroes have any more (Inaudible) than white people, that therefore a greater percentage of Negroes would asked to be excused than would white. And as to the inference that might be raised by such a situation as you have in Jefferson County with such a small number of people, being -- Negroes being called for jury service over a long period of time. This Court said, in another decision coming out of Texas that it should tax the credulity of the Court, to say that mere chance resulted in such few Negroes being called for jury service in a county, where you have such a high percentage of Negroes making up the population and a high percentage of qualified voters. Another question was directed to the Attorney General as to whether or not in Jefferson County you would find an unusually high percentage of illiterate colored people in that county. Now I've lived in that county all in my life. Insofar as Negroes are concerned, Jefferson County is the leading cultural and educational county of the State of Arkansas for Negroes. It is the home of the Arkansas State College for Negroes. It is the home of (Inaudible) Institute for Negroes. For many, many years the Negro High School has been accredited by the North Central Association. And I dare say that percentage wise there is a greater percentage of literate Negroes in Jefferson County than any other county in the State of Arkansas, and that the literacy qualifications of Negroes is probably equal to or superior percentage wise than the literacy test of white people in that county because there is not a single white college within that county. And certainly anytime that you have a county where you've had a college down through the years, there generally is a higher literacy portion in that particular county than you have in a county where you do not have a college.
